title: Volumetric Godray Shader Studio with Duck Subject
description: The user controls a volumetric godray shader environment where a Duck model serves as the light-occluding subject. Users manipulate shader uniforms (ray density, decay, weight, exposure, color tint) across multiple coupled light sources extracted from the godrays demo scene, with an energy conservation law ensuring total light energy remains constant when redistributing intensity between sources. A full undo/redo history tracks all parameter changes.
prompt:
Create a single HTML file using Three.js r170 that loads both godrays_demo.glb and Duck.glb via GLTFLoader from a shared_assets folder. Place the Duck model at the scene center as the primary occluding object for a custom screen-space godray post-processing shader. Extract or create 3 point light sources inspired by the godrays_demo scene geometry, positioned around the Duck at 120-degree intervals. Implement a multi-pass godray shader: Pass 1 renders an occlusion map (black Duck silhouette against bright light sources), Pass 2 applies radial blur sampling from each light's screen-space position with uniforms for density (0.1-2.0), weight (0.01-1.0), decay (0.9-1.0), and exposure (0.1-3.0). Pass 3 composites godrays onto the main scene with additive blending and a user-selectable RGB color tint per light source. Enforce an energy conservation law: the sum of all 3 lights' exposure values must equal a constant (e.g., 3.0). When the user increases one light's exposure via a slider, automatically redistribute the deficit proportionally among the other two lights, updating their sliders reactively. Provide a UI panel with sliders for each light's density, weight, decay, exposure, and a color picker for tint. Add a master decay slider that couples all three lights' decay values with individual offsets. Implement undo/redo buttons that track every parameter change in a history stack (minimum 50 entries). Clicking a light source in the 3D viewport selects it and highlights its UI section. The user can orbit the camera around the Duck with OrbitControls. The camera starts at position (0, 2, 5) looking at origin. Use a dark background to make godrays visible. The godrays_demo geometry should appear as decorative architectural framing elements around the scene edges. All shader code must be inline GLSL strings in script tags.

[State Exposure (required)]
Expose window.__3D_STATE__ updated every animation frame:
window.__3D_STATE__ = {
  light1Exposure: number,         // exposure value for first godray light source (0.1-3.0)
  light2Exposure: number,         // exposure value for second godray light source (0.1-3.0)
  light3Exposure: number,         // exposure value for third godray light source (0.1-3.0)
  rayDensity: number,         // global ray sampling density for radial blur (0.1-2.0)
  rayWeight: number,         // weight factor for godray intensity (0.01-1.0)
  rayDecay: number,         // decay rate along ray samples (0.9-1.0)
  light1ColorR: number,         // red channel for light 1 tint (0-1)
  light2ColorG: number,         // green channel for light 2 tint (0-1)
  light3ColorB: number,         // blue channel for light 3 tint (0-1)
  duckRotationY: number,         // Y-axis rotation of Duck model in radians
  energyConserved: boolean,         // whether total exposure equals conservation constant (3.0)
  assetsLoaded: boolean,         // whether both GLB models have loaded successfully
}
